heroOverview: Cleft lip and palate is the most common craniofacial birth defect worldwide, occurring in approximately 1 in 700 live births — around 175,000 new cases annually. It results from incomplete fusion of the embryonic facial processes between the fourth and tenth weeks of gestation, producing a gap in the upper lip, the roof of the mouth, or both. The condition ranges from a small notch in the lip border to a complete bilateral cleft extending through the alveolar ridge and hard and soft palate. With modern coordinated cleft team care — involving maxillofacial surgery, plastic surgery, orthodontics, speech therapy, and audiology — the vast majority of affected individuals achieve near-normal facial appearance, intelligible speech, and good quality of life. Early surgical repair in the first 12 months of life is the foundation of treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 1 in 700 live births globally; about 2,650 infants born with cleft lip and/or palate in the UK annually (CRANE registry); roughly 7,000 per year in the United States
keyStats.demographics: Cleft lip with or without palate is more common in males (2:1); isolated cleft palate is slightly more common in females; highest incidence in Asian and Native American populations, lowest in Black African populations
keyStats.avgAge: Present from birth; diagnosis increasingly made by prenatal ultrasound from 18-20 weeks gestation
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 175,000 infants born with orofacial clefts worldwide each year (WHO, 2023)

definition: Cleft lip and palate (ICD-10: Q35–Q37) is a congenital structural anomaly caused by failure of the embryonic facial processes — specifically the median nasal process and the maxillary processes — to fuse completely during weeks 4-10 of intrauterine development. The cleft lip (cheiloschisis) arises from incomplete fusion of the philtrum columns, while the cleft palate (palatoschisis) results from failure of the palatal shelves to fuse along the midline between weeks 7 and 10. These processes may occur together (cleft lip and palate, the most common combined form) or in isolation. Isolated cleft palate represents a distinct embryological and genetic entity, with different risk factors and a different associated syndrome profile. Aetiology is multifactorial: a combination of genetic variants (mutations in IRF6, MSX1, MTHFR, BRCA3P, TBX22, and over 50 other genes implicated to date) and environmental exposures (folate deficiency, anticonvulsant use, maternal smoking, and alcohol) interact to disrupt normal facial development. About 30% of orofacial clefts occur as part of a recognised syndrome (e.g. Van der Woude syndrome, velocardiofacial syndrome, Pierre Robin sequence); the remainder are non-syndromic. Management requires a multidisciplinary cleft team over many years — from neonatal feeding support through primary surgical repairs in infancy, secondary bone grafting in middle childhood, orthodontics, and, in some cases, orthognathic surgery in adulthood. The specialty responsible is maxillofacial and oral surgery, working alongside plastic surgery, paediatric dentistry, orthodontics, speech-language pathology, ENT, audiology, and clinical psychology.

typesClassification.description: A gap limited to the upper lip, ranging from a small notch in the vermilion border (microform or forme fruste) to a complete unilateral or bilateral cleft extending up to the base of the nose; the palate is intact.

Incomplete fusion of the palatal shelves without any lip cleft, ranging from a bifid uvula to a full cleft of the soft and hard palate; this is a distinct embryological entity more commonly associated with syndromes such as Pierre Robin sequence.
The most common combined form; the cleft runs on one side from the lip through the alveolar ridge (gum) into the hard and soft palate, producing characteristic nasal deformity on the affected side.

Clefts on both sides of the lip and palate, producing the most severe functional and aesthetic disruption; the premaxilla is often prominent and mobile, and feeding difficulties are most marked in this group.

The mucosal lining of the palate is intact but the underlying muscle and/or bone are cleft; the classic triad is a bifid uvula, a blue midline zone (zona pellucida), and a notch at the posterior hard palate. Often diagnosed late because the palate looks superficially normal.

primarySymptoms: Visible gap in the upper lip — the most immediately apparent feature at birth, ranging from a notch in the lip border to a wide bilateral split reaching both nostrils.
Opening in the roof of the mouth — when the palate is involved, a gap or groove running from behind the alveolar ridge into the soft palate is visible on examination of the infant's mouth.

Difficulty feeding in newborns — the cleft disrupts the oral seal needed to generate negative suction pressure, so affected infants cannot breastfeed or bottle-feed normally without a specialised cleft or squeezable bottle.
Milk and liquid regurgitating through the nose during feeding — caused by the direct communication between the oral and nasal cavities when the palate is cleft.

Recurrent middle ear infections (otitis media with effusion, 'glue ear') — the tensor veli palatini muscle is disrupted, impairing Eustachian tube function and leading to fluid accumulation in the middle ear; occurs in 80-90% of children with cleft palate.
Hearing loss — conductive hearing loss resulting from recurrent or chronic glue ear is present in the majority of children before palate repair and affects speech and language development.

Speech difficulties after palate repair — particularly velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI), in which air escapes through the nose during speech, producing hypernasality and reduced intelligibility in up to 20-30% of children after primary palate repair.
Dental anomalies — missing, supernumerary, or malformed teeth are common in the cleft region; in complete unilateral or bilateral clefts, the alveolar ridge is split, disrupting tooth eruption.
Nasal deformity — asymmetry of the nasal tip, alar base, and columella, particularly in unilateral clefts, resulting from disrupted nasal cartilage and muscle attachment.
Psychosocial difficulties — school-age children and adolescents have measurably higher rates of anxiety, low self-esteem, and peer teasing related to facial appearance and speech.
earlyWarningSigns: Detection of a facial cleft on routine antenatal ultrasound at 18-20 weeks, allowing prenatal counselling and delivery planning at a specialist centre.
Inability to achieve a seal during initial breastfeeding attempts in the newborn, leading to poor weight gain.
Audible nasal escape of milk during bottle feeding before any formal diagnosis has been made.
A bifid uvula noticed during a routine newborn check, which should prompt examination of the palatal midline to exclude a submucous cleft.
emergencySigns: Severe respiratory distress in a newborn with Pierre Robin sequence (micrognathia, glossoptosis, and cleft palate), where the tongue falls back to obstruct the airway — requires immediate prone positioning and possible airway intervention.
Failure to thrive in the first weeks of life with inadequate weight gain despite feeding support, indicating the infant is not taking sufficient calories.
Signs of aspiration pneumonia — respiratory distress, persistent cough, wheeze, or fever in an infant with a cleft palate — requires same-day paediatric assessment.
Acute post-operative bleeding from the lip or palate repair site, particularly in the first 24-48 hours after surgery.

causes.description: Over 50 genetic loci have been associated with non-syndromic cleft lip and palate, including variants in IRF6, MSX1, MTHFR, BRCA3P, and FGFR genes. First-degree relatives of an affected child carry a 3-4% recurrence risk, rising to 15% if two or more relatives are affected, consistent with a polygenic threshold model.

Low folate intake before and during early pregnancy has been consistently linked to increased risk of orofacial clefts through its essential role in neural tube closure and midline facial development. Periconceptional folic acid supplementation (0.4-4 mg daily) reduces risk in population studies by approximately 25-35%.

Prenatal tobacco smoke exposure increases the risk of cleft lip and palate by approximately 30-70% in large cohort studies, with a dose-response relationship; the mechanism involves hypoxia, reduced antioxidant capacity, and nicotine-related vasoconstriction impairing facial tissue vascularisation during development.

Certain anticonvulsant medications — particularly phenytoin, valproate, topiramate, and lamotrigine when used in the first trimester — carry a 2-9 fold elevated risk of orofacial clefts. Retinoids, corticosteroids, and methotrexate are also associated with increased risk.

Heavy alcohol use in the first trimester interferes with retinoic acid signalling and neural crest cell migration, both critical for midline facial fusion, and is associated with an approximately twofold elevated cleft risk; fetal alcohol spectrum disorder includes midline facial defects.

About 30% of cleft cases are components of recognised syndromes. Van der Woude syndrome (IRF6 mutation, autosomal dominant) accounts for 2% of all cleft lip and palate cases; velocardiofacial syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) commonly includes cleft palate; Pierre Robin sequence, Treacher Collins syndrome, and over 300 other syndromes list cleft palate among their features.

Pre-gestational diabetes (both type 1 and type 2) is associated with a 1.5-2 fold increase in orofacial cleft risk, possibly through hyperglycaemia-induced oxidative stress disrupting facial morphogenesis during sensitive windows of development.

riskFactors.description: Having a parent, sibling, or second-degree relative with a cleft lip or palate raises the child's risk to approximately 3-4% (from a baseline of 0.14%); two affected first-degree relatives increases the risk to about 15%.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex (for cleft lip with or without palate)
riskFactors.description: Cleft lip with or without palate is approximately twice as common in males as females, reflecting sex-linked differences in palatal shelf fusion timing and width.

Inadequate periconceptional folate intake is the most clearly modifiable risk factor; all women planning pregnancy should take 0.4 mg of folic acid daily, starting at least one month before conception.
Prenatal tobacco exposure carries a dose-dependent increase in cleft risk; stopping smoking before or very early in pregnancy reduces this risk back toward baseline.

Anticonvulsants (especially valproate, phenytoin, topiramate), retinoids, and other teratogens taken in weeks 4-10 of pregnancy carry significant cleft risk; women of childbearing age on these medications need pre-conception counselling about alternative agents.

Asian and Native American populations have the highest orofacial cleft rates (1 in 500); Black African populations have the lowest (1 in 2,500); European populations are intermediate at approximately 1 in 700.
Pre-gestational diabetes carries a 1.5-2 fold cleft risk; optimising glycaemic control before conception and through the first trimester reduces but may not eliminate this risk.

affectedDemographics: Newborn infants globally; diagnosis is at birth or prenatally by ultrasound
Males more often than females for cleft lip with or without cleft palate
Families with a history of orofacial cleft in a first-degree relative
Children of mothers who smoked, took anticonvulsants, or had poorly controlled diabetes in early pregnancy
Asian and Indigenous populations, who have the highest prevalence rates globally
Children with syndromic conditions such as velocardiofacial syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) or Van der Woude syndrome
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cleft lip is increasingly made by prenatal ultrasound at the 18-20 week anomaly scan, with detection rates for cleft lip of 25-75% at tertiary centres, though isolated cleft palate is far less reliably identified prenatally and is still most often diagnosed at birth. Prenatal detection allows preparation of the family, planning for delivery at a centre with neonatal cleft expertise, and timely specialist counselling. At birth, clinical inspection of the lip, nose, alveolus, and palate — using adequate lighting and gentle depression of the tongue — confirms the diagnosis and characterises the extent of the cleft. The palate must always be palpated and directly visualised to exclude a submucous cleft. Once the diagnosis is established, the multidisciplinary cleft team performs a comprehensive assessment including otological examination (because virtually all children with cleft palate develop glue ear), audiological testing, and chromosomal or genetic analysis when a syndrome is suspected. Prenatal MRI can be used when ultrasound findings suggest associated brain or other structural anomalies. The Kernahan-Stark classification or the Veau system is used to document cleft extent for surgical planning. Genetic counselling is offered to all families to discuss recurrence risk, and FISH or chromosomal microarray is considered when dysmorphic features suggest a syndromic diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects cleft lip (and occasionally cleft palate) prenatally, enabling family counselling, referral to a multidisciplinary cleft team, and delivery planning at an appropriate centre.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine mid-pregnancy ultrasound; the sonographer specifically examines the fetal lip and palate. No additional preparation needed. A suspected cleft triggers referral to a tertiary fetal medicine unit for detailed scanning.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical inspection and palatal palpation at birth
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms and characterises the cleft at the neonatal examination, specifically to identify submucous cleft palate, which may be missed without deliberate palpation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A midwife, neonatologist, or paediatrician visually examines and palpates the baby's lip, gum ridge, and palate within the first hours of life; entirely painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for glue ear (otitis media with effusion), which occurs in 80-90% of children with cleft palate and causes conductive hearing loss that can delay speech development if untreated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small probe placed gently at the ear canal entrance measures eardrum mobility; takes about a minute per ear and is painless. Grommets (ventilation tubes) are commonly inserted at the time of palate repair.

diagnosticTests.test: Audiological assessment (distraction testing / ABR in infants)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the conductive hearing loss associated with Eustachian tube dysfunction and middle ear effusion, guiding hearing aid or grommet decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: In newborns, auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing uses scalp electrodes and earphones to detect brain responses to sound clicks; in older infants, distraction and play-based tests are used.

diagnosticTests.test: Chromosomal microarray or FISH for 22q11.2 deletion
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out velocardiofacial syndrome (DiGeorge syndrome), which is the most common syndrome associated with cleft palate and carries significant cardiac, immune, and neurodevelopmental implications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is sent for chromosomal analysis; results take 2-4 weeks. Ordered when the child has any dysmorphic features beyond the cleft or a cardiac defect.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates velopharyngeal insufficiency — the failure of the soft palate to adequately close the nasal airway during speech — which affects 20-30% of children after palate repair and may require further surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine flexible camera is passed through the nostril under local anaesthetic spray to visualise the velum during speech; the child reads or repeats sentences while the clinician observes palatal movement.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of cleft lip and palate follows a structured multidisciplinary protocol spanning from birth to adulthood. In the neonatal period, a cleft nurse specialist teaches parents specialist feeding techniques (Haberman, Medela SpecialNeeds, or Mead-Johnson bottles) and arranges early multidisciplinary team review. Pre-surgical nasoalveolar moulding (PNAM) — a series of custom oral plates that reshape the alveolar ridges and nasal cartilages before lip repair — is used at many centres to improve surgical outcomes in complete unilateral and bilateral clefts. Primary lip repair (cheiloplasty) is performed at approximately 3-6 months of age. The most widely used technique for unilateral cleft is the Millard rotation-advancement repair or the Mohler modification; bilateral clefts are repaired using the Mulliken or Manchester technique. Primary palate repair (palatoplasty) is performed at 6-12 months and aims to close the palate, restore normal oral-nasal separation, and reconstruct the levator veli palatini muscle sling that is essential for speech and Eustachian tube function. Grommets (ventilation tubes) are inserted at the same anaesthetic if there is active glue ear. Alveolar bone grafting — placing cancellous bone from the iliac crest into the alveolar cleft — is performed at approximately 8-10 years when the canine tooth is erupting, to provide bone support for the developing dentition. Ongoing orthodontic treatment is required from mixed dentition through to the completion of growth. About 20-30% of patients require secondary speech surgery (pharyngoplasty or sphincter pharyngoplasty) for persistent velopharyngeal insufficiency. Rhinoplasty and/or orthognathic surgery may be offered to skeletally mature patients (aged 18+) who have residual nasal deformity or maxillary hypoplasia — a midfacial retrusion seen in about 25-30% of repaired cleft palate patients resulting from surgical scarring affecting midface growth.

medicalTreatments.name: Specialist feeding support and adaptive bottle feeding
medicalTreatments.description: Cleft nurse specialists teach parents how to use a Haberman or Mead-Johnson squeezable bottle that delivers milk with minimal oral suction effort. Feeding support in the first weeks of life prevents malnutrition and ensures adequate caloric intake before surgery. Breastfeeding is not possible in the presence of a palatal cleft but may be possible for isolated cleft lip after repair.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured feeding programmes restore adequate weight gain in approximately 90-95% of infants within 2 weeks of specialist support.

medicalTreatments.name: Grommet (ventilation tube) insertion for glue ear
medicalTreatments.description: Myringotomy with ventilation tube insertion drains middle ear fluid and restores normal middle ear pressure, resolving conductive hearing loss. Performed under general anaesthetic, usually at the same time as palate repair. Multiple sets of grommets may be required as children grow.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores near-normal hearing thresholds in approximately 85-90% of ears with active middle ear effusion; reduces re-hospitalisation for ear disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for recurrent otitis media
medicalTreatments.description: Children with cleft palate are prone to acute otitis media in addition to glue ear; short antibiotic courses (amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate) treat acute episodes and are given prophylactically in selected high-recurrence cases between grommet sets.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Amoxicillin achieves bacterial cure in approximately 80-90% of acute otitis media episodes; prophylactic antibiotics reduce recurrence by about 50% in highly recurrent cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech and language therapy (SLT)
medicalTreatments.description: Intensive SLT begins in the second year of life, targeting articulation, phoneme development, and resonance. In children with VPI after palate repair, SLT addresses compensatory articulation patterns and helps determine whether secondary speech surgery is needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured early SLT improves speech intelligibility scores by a clinically meaningful degree in observational studies; its combination with surgery produces better outcomes than either alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Periconceptional folic acid supplementation (prevention)
medicalTreatments.description: Folic acid 0.4 mg daily, taken at least one month before conception and through the first trimester, reduces the risk of neural tube defects and is associated with a 25-35% reduction in non-syndromic orofacial cleft risk across population studies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cleft risk by approximately 25-35% in women without previous affected offspring; high-dose folic acid (4 mg) is used for women with a prior affected child or on anticonvulsants.

surgicalOptions.name: Primary lip repair (cheiloplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed at 3-6 months of age, this operation closes the cleft in the lip and reconstructs the orbicularis oris muscle ring. Techniques include Millard rotation-advancement, Mohler modification, and Tennison-Randall for unilateral clefts; Mulliken or Manchester techniques for bilateral clefts.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent cosmetic and functional results in over 90% of cases; about 10-20% require minor lip or nasal revision in later childhood or adulthood.

surgicalOptions.name: Primary palate repair (palatoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed at 6-12 months to close the palatal defect and reconstruct the levator veli palatini muscle sling. The two main techniques are the von Langenbeck repair and the Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty (which provides a longer velum and lower VPI rates).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Furlow palatoplasty achieves normal speech resonance in 70-80% of patients; VPI requiring further surgery affects 20-30%.

surgicalOptions.name: Alveolar bone graft
surgicalOptions.description: Cancellous bone from the anterior iliac crest is packed into the alveolar cleft at age 8-10 years during the period of mixed dentition, providing bone support for the erupting canine tooth and closing the residual oronasal fistula in the gum.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft success (bone fill grade I or II on panoramic X-ray) achieved in approximately 80-90% of cases at experienced cleft centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Secondary speech surgery (pharyngoplasty or sphincter pharyngoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: For persistent velopharyngeal insufficiency causing hypernasality and nasal air escape, a posterior pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty narrows the velopharyngeal port to improve speech resonance.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70-90% of patients achieve normal or near-normal resonance after secondary speech surgery; obstructive sleep apnoea is a known risk requiring monitoring.

surgicalOptions.name: Orthognathic surgery (Le Fort I osteotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: In adolescents and adults with midfacial retrusion secondary to surgical scarring, a Le Fort I maxillary advancement restores facial balance and bite relationship; performed only when facial growth is complete, typically after age 16-18.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Skeletal and dental correction achieved in approximately 90-95% of properly selected patients; relapse occurs in 10-20% and may require retreatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-surgical nasoalveolar moulding (PNAM)
alternativeTreatments.description: Custom-made intraoral appliances worn from birth to the time of lip repair gently remodel the alveolar ridges and nasal cartilages. Evidence from prospective studies shows PNAM reduces the width of the alveolar cleft and improves nasal symmetry, potentially reducing the extent of surgical correction needed at primary lip repair.

alternativeTreatments.name: Obturator or palatal plate in infancy
alternativeTreatments.description: Before palate repair, a removable hard palate plate can be fitted to cover the cleft, improving feeding efficiency and potentially supporting palatal arch development. Used at some but not all cleft centres; evidence on long-term developmental benefit remains mixed.

whySeeSpecialist: Cleft lip and palate mandates management by a dedicated multidisciplinary cleft team from birth. In the UK, care is centralised at commissioned cleft services; in the US, the American Cleft Palate-Craniofacial Association (ACPA) accredits interdisciplinary teams. Non-specialist management significantly increases the rates of speech problems, facial growth restriction, and dental complications. Specific specialist referrals include: clinical genetics for syndromic cases, paediatric cardiology if a 22q11.2 deletion is identified, and specialist orthodontics/orthognathic surgery for jaw discrepancies in adolescence.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an ACPA-approved cleft centre (in the US) or an NHS-commissioned cleft service (in the UK), where surgeon volume is high — outcomes improve markedly above approximately 100 cleft repairs per year per surgeon. Verify the team includes cleft nursing, speech-language pathology, orthodontics, audiology, and clinical psychology. Ask how many primary lip and palate repairs the centre performs annually, what their VPI rate is, and whether they participate in national outcome registries such as CRANE (UK) or the National Cleft Registry (US).
preventionStrategies: Take 0.4 mg of folic acid daily, beginning at least one month before trying to conceive and continuing through the first trimester; women with a previous affected child or taking anticonvulsants should take 4 mg under medical supervision.
Avoid smoking throughout pregnancy; tobacco use carries a 30-70% increase in cleft risk with a dose-response relationship.
Limit alcohol consumption — the safest level in pregnancy is none, and heavy first-trimester use significantly raises orofacial cleft risk.
Discuss any medications — particularly anticonvulsants, retinoids, and methotrexate — with a clinician before and during pregnancy to identify lower-risk alternatives.
Optimise glycaemic control if you have diabetes before conception and throughout the first trimester.
Attend the 18-20 week anomaly scan, which offers prenatal detection and allows preparation for delivery at a specialist centre.
lifestyleModifications: Parents of affected newborns should attend specialist cleft feeding clinics within days of birth to learn appropriate feeding technique and prevent weight loss.
Keep all cleft team appointments throughout childhood; the treatment protocol spans 18+ years with critical timing windows for each intervention.
Ensure hearing is tested and monitored regularly, as glue ear and conductive hearing loss affect language development and school readiness.
Support the child's speech development with early speech therapy from age 18 months and monitor for signs of VPI such as hypernasality.
Prepare children and adolescents for the social challenges of visible difference; early referral to clinical psychology reduces rates of anxiety and low self-esteem.
Maintain meticulous dental hygiene; the cleft region has higher rates of dental caries and missing teeth and requires closer dental surveillance.

recommended: High-calorie expressed breast milk or formula delivered via specialist cleft bottle to meet growth requirements in the newborn period
Soft, easily chewed foods during periods before and after alveolar bone graft surgery
Adequate folic acid and iron-rich foods during pregnancy for prevention in future pregnancies
Regular dental check-ups with fluoride application to protect teeth that are more caries-prone in the cleft region
avoid: Hard, crunchy, or chewy foods for 4-6 weeks after palate repair to protect the surgical wound
Hot foods and liquids for several weeks after palate repair until mucosal healing is complete
Smoking and alcohol during pregnancy to minimise teratogenic risk in future pregnancies

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions for children or adults with cleft lip and palate who have completed surgical repair. Full participation in sports and physical education is encouraged. After any surgical intervention, activity is restricted for 4-6 weeks as directed by the surgical team to protect wound integrity. Children with associated 22q11.2 deletion should be assessed for cardiac anomalies before participating in vigorous competitive sport.
dailyManagement: Follow the cleft team's post-operative instructions precisely — arm restraints, soft diet, and wound care after lip or palate repair directly affect healing and long-term results.
Maintain consistent speech therapy exercises at home between clinic visits; parental involvement in SLT programmes significantly improves speech outcomes.
Monitor hearing at each school year; notify the GP or cleft team if the child seems to be missing speech sounds, has delayed language, or performs poorly at school.
Attend regular orthodontic reviews from age 7 to prepare for bone graft timing and manage arch development.
Access cleft support organisations for peer connection, information, and advocacy: CLAPA (UK) and Cleft Palate Foundation (US) provide parent networks and emotional support.
prognosis: With modern multidisciplinary cleft team care, outcomes for children born with cleft lip and palate are excellent in terms of both function and appearance. The large majority achieve intelligible speech, normal hearing, and a satisfying facial appearance. About 70-80% of children have normal or near-normal speech after primary palate repair without requiring secondary surgery; the remainder can achieve good speech with pharyngoplasty. Long-term dental outcomes depend on compliance with orthodontic treatment and the success of alveolar bone grafting. Midfacial retrusion requiring orthognathic surgery develops in 25-30% of patients as a result of surgical scarring affecting midface growth. Psychological outcomes are generally positive when families receive adequate support; affected individuals have normal quality-of-life scores in most domains by adulthood. Recurrence risk in offspring of affected individuals is approximately 3-4% for non-syndromic cases.
recoveryTimeline: After primary lip repair at 3-6 months, swelling and bruising resolve over 2-4 weeks and scar maturation takes 6-12 months. After palate repair at 6-12 months, the palate heals over 4-6 weeks; the child returns to a normal diet after 4-6 weeks. Alveolar bone graft at age 8-10 has a recovery of 4-6 weeks with restricted diet. Secondary speech surgery (pharyngoplasty) requires a one to two night inpatient stay and a soft diet for 2-3 weeks. Orthognathic surgery in adolescence or adulthood has a 6-12 week post-operative period with restricted diet and significant facial swelling, with final results apparent at 6-12 months as bone consolidates.
complications: Velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) — occurs in 20-30% after palate repair; produces persistent hypernasality and nasal air escape during speech, requiring assessment and often secondary speech surgery.
Conductive hearing loss from chronic glue ear — affects the majority of children with cleft palate before and after repair; requires audiological monitoring, grommets, and occasionally hearing aids.
Dental and orthodontic problems — missing lateral incisors, supernumerary teeth, alveolar cleft, and dental crowding requiring complex orthodontic management spanning years.
Midfacial retrusion (midface hypoplasia) — in 25-30% of repaired cleft palate patients, surgical scarring restricts midfacial growth, requiring Le Fort I advancement in adulthood.
Oronasal fistula — a persistent communication between the mouth and nose after palate repair, causing nasal regurgitation of liquid and nasal speech; requires surgical revision.

Psychosocial difficulties — teasing, social anxiety, and low self-esteem in adolescence are measurably more common than in the general population; early psychological support reduces long-term impact.

supportResources.name: CLAPA — Cleft Lip and Palate Association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, peer support, and advocacy for individuals and families affected by cleft lip and palate.

supportResources.name: Cleft Palate Foundation (US)
supportResources.description: US organisation offering educational resources, team finder, and support networks for patients, families, and clinicians.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cleft lip and palate
supportResources.description: NHS patient guide on diagnosis, treatment pathway, and support available in the UK.

sources.title: Dixon MJ et al. Cleft lip and palate: understanding genetic and environmental influences. Nature Reviews Genetics, 2011
Mossey PA et al. Cleft lip and palate. The Lancet, 2009
Cobourne MT. The complex genetics of cleft lip and palate. European Journal of Orthodontics, 2004
NICE — Interventions for children with cleft palate: speech outcomes (2016 evidence review)
American Cleft Palate-Craniofacial Association — Parameters for the Evaluation and Treatment of Patients with Cleft Lip/Palate, 2018
NHS — CRANE Database: cleft care audit UK
Wehby GL et al. Folic acid and orofacial clefts: a systematic review of the literature. Oral Diseases, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Oral cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Oral cancer is an acquired malignancy that develops in adults, presenting with a non-healing ulcer or growth in the mouth, whereas cleft lip and palate is a congenital structural defect present from birth.

confusedWithConditions.name: Laryngopharyngeal reflux
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Laryngopharyngeal reflux causes chronic throat clearing, hoarseness, and dysphagia in adults from acid irritation and is not structurally visible, unlike the visible midline gap of a palatal cleft in an infant.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sleep apnoea produces nocturnal breathing pauses from airway collapse and is a functional disorder in adults; cleft palate in Pierre Robin sequence causes neonatal airway obstruction from a structural tongue-base issue requiring different management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Parotitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Parotitis is an inflammatory or infectious swelling of the parotid gland presenting as cheek and jaw swelling with facial pain in a patient of any age, entirely distinct from the midline structural defect of a cleft lip or palate.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic otitis media
relatedConditions.relevance: Eustachian tube dysfunction from palatal muscle disruption causes otitis media with effusion in 80-90% of children with cleft palate, requiring ongoing audiological management.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Pharyngoplasty for VPI can narrow the airway and induce or worsen obstructive sleep apnoea; OSA may also be pre-existing in Pierre Robin sequence.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngopharyngeal reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: Altered pharyngeal anatomy after palate repair can contribute to reflux symptoms affecting voice and swallowing in a subset of treated patients.

relatedConditions.name: Oral cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Adults who have undergone cleft repairs have altered oropharyngeal anatomy and should maintain regular dental and oral mucosal screening for premalignant change.

faqs.question: Is a cleft lip and palate serious?
faqs.answer: It is a significant birth defect that affects feeding, hearing, speech, dental development, and facial appearance, but with coordinated specialist team care from birth, the great majority of affected children achieve normal speech, hearing, and a satisfying appearance by adulthood.

faqs.question: Can a cleft lip and palate be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Cleft lip is detected on the routine 18-20 week ultrasound scan in about 25-75% of cases at specialist centres. Isolated cleft palate without lip involvement is much harder to see on ultrasound and is usually diagnosed at birth. Prenatal detection allows specialist counselling and delivery planning.

faqs.question: When is surgery performed for cleft lip and palate?
faqs.answer: Lip repair is performed at approximately 3-6 months of age. Palate repair follows at 6-12 months. Alveolar bone grafting is done around ages 8-10 years. Secondary speech surgery, orthodontic treatment, and possible orthognathic jaw surgery are completed through adolescence and into adulthood.

faqs.question: Can a baby with cleft lip and palate breastfeed?
faqs.answer: Breastfeeding is not possible when the palate is cleft because the infant cannot generate the suction pressure needed. Specialist squeezable bottles (Haberman or Mead-Johnson) allow adequate feeding. After lip-only repair, breastfeeding is often possible. A cleft nurse specialist teaches the technique in the first days of life.

faqs.question: Will my child have normal speech?
faqs.answer: About 70-80% of children develop normal or near-normal speech after primary palate repair. About 20-30% develop velopharyngeal insufficiency causing a nasal quality to speech, which can be addressed with further surgery and speech therapy. Early and consistent speech therapy from age 18 months significantly improves outcomes.

faqs.question: Is cleft lip and palate hereditary?
faqs.answer: It has a genetic component, but most cases are non-syndromic and multifactorial — not caused by a single gene. First-degree relatives of an affected person carry a 3-4% risk (versus 0.14% in the general population). If two close relatives are affected, the risk rises to about 15%. Genetic counselling can provide personalised estimates.

faqs.question: What causes cleft lip and palate?
faqs.answer: Most cases result from a combination of multiple genetic variants and environmental factors — including folate deficiency, maternal smoking, certain anticonvulsant medications, and poorly controlled diabetes — interacting to disrupt facial fusion during weeks 4-10 of pregnancy. About 30% are part of a recognised genetic syndrome.

faqs.question: Will my child need hearing aids?
faqs.answer: Most children with cleft palate develop glue ear from Eustachian tube dysfunction and need grommets (ventilation tubes) inserted at the time of palate repair to restore hearing. If grommets are not sufficient, hearing aids may be needed temporarily. Hearing normalises in most cases once palate muscle function is restored.

faqs.question: How can I prevent cleft lip and palate?
faqs.answer: Take 0.4 mg of folic acid daily from at least one month before conception through the first trimester. Avoid smoking, alcohol, and teratogenic medications in early pregnancy. Optimise blood sugar control if you have diabetes before conception. These measures reduce but cannot eliminate risk because genetic factors also play a role.

faqs.question: What is velopharyngeal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) occurs when the soft palate cannot completely close off the nasal cavity during speech, allowing air to escape through the nose. It produces a nasal quality to the voice and is present after palate repair in about 20-30% of children. It is assessed by nasopharyngoscopy and treated with further surgery.

faqs.question: Are there dental problems associated with cleft palate?
faqs.answer: Yes. The cleft commonly passes through the alveolar ridge (gum), causing missing or extra teeth in the cleft area, delayed eruption, and crowding. Alveolar bone grafting at age 8-10 years provides bone to support the erupting canine tooth. Regular orthodontic care is needed from the mixed dentition stage.

faqs.question: Will my child need to see many different specialists?
faqs.answer: Yes, but at a centralised cleft team where all specialists collaborate and review the child together at key ages — birth, 1 year, 5 years, 10 years, 15 years, and 20 years. The team typically includes a surgeon, orthodontist, speech-language therapist, audiologist, ENT, clinical psychologist, and cleft nurse coordinator.

faqs.question: Does cleft palate affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: Cleft lip and palate itself does not affect intelligence. A small subset of children with associated genetic syndromes (such as 22q11.2 deletion) may have learning difficulties, but these arise from the underlying syndrome, not the cleft. Most children with isolated cleft have normal intellectual development.

faqs.question: What is Pierre Robin sequence?
faqs.answer: Pierre Robin sequence is the triad of small jaw (micrognathia), tongue falling back (glossoptosis), and cleft palate. The tongue obstructs the airway at birth, potentially requiring prone positioning, nasopharyngeal airway, or surgical jaw distraction in severe cases. It can be isolated or part of a syndrome.

faqs.question: How many operations will my child need?
faqs.answer: Most children with complete cleft lip and palate undergo primary lip repair, primary palate repair, and alveolar bone graft — three planned operations. About 20-30% need secondary speech surgery, and about 25-30% need orthognathic surgery in adulthood. Minor revisions to the lip or nose are common.

faqs.question: When can scars from cleft repair be revised?
faqs.answer: Lip scar revision is generally deferred until the child is at least 5-6 years old and the scar has fully matured (at least 12-18 months after surgery). Definitive nasal correction is delayed until facial growth is complete, usually at age 16-18. Many families choose revision in adolescence for psychosocial benefit.

faqs.question: How does cleft palate affect the ears?
faqs.answer: The levator veli palatini muscle, which opens the Eustachian tube during swallowing, is disrupted by the cleft. This impairs middle ear ventilation and causes fluid build-up (glue ear) in 80-90% of affected children, leading to conductive hearing loss. Grommet insertion at palate repair time restores normal middle ear drainage.

faqs.question: Is there psychological support available?
faqs.answer: Yes. All accredited cleft teams include or refer to a clinical psychologist. Organisations such as CLAPA in the UK and the Cleft Palate Foundation in the US provide peer support groups, parent networks, and online resources. Early psychological support significantly reduces rates of social anxiety and low self-esteem in affected adolescents.

metaTitle: Cleft Lip and Palate: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Cleft lip and palate affects 1 in 700 births, disrupting feeding, speech and hearing. Learn about surgery, team care and outcomes. Doctor-reviewed guide.
